title: Function reference
text:
The high-level entry point: fit the ancestry CTMC on the reference tips and paint the query haplotypes. paint returns a Painting; fit exposes the EM directly.



paint
Infer soft local ancestry along query haplotypes from a tree sequence.


Painting
Result of :func:paint: the soft local-ancestry posteriors plus the fitted model.


fit
Blocked EM for (Q, π, {w_i}) (CLAUDE.md §3, §11.1.5-6).


FitResult
Fitted parameters returned by :func:fit.






The soft per-haplotype posterior (Segment tracks) and its collapse into hard ancestry tracts for downstream tract-length / admixture-dating analysis.



output.posterior_table
Per-sample ancestry posterior as contiguous segments covering [0, L).


output.hard_segments
Collapse a soft posterior track into hard ancestry segments.


output.Segment
A contiguous span carrying one soft ancestry posterior.


output.missing_info_mask
Per-sample spans where the tree is uninformative (isolated sample).


output.posterior_at
Posterior vector for sample at a genomic position.






Audit a reference panel for admixture / mislabelling, flag foreign tracts of any haplotype without naming a donor, detect introgression from an unsampled (“ghost”) source, and — with no reference at all — detect deep archaic tracts (the tspaint.introgression and tspaint.archaic namespaces). All read the leave-one-out map, which surfaces a sample’s own foreign tracts the down-pass posterior hides; detect_ghost and detect_archaic return objects with a shared .burden / .tracts().



reference_qc
Audit a reference panel for admixture / mislabelling (Plan A Workflow 1; CLAUDE.md §9).


foreign_tracts
Anonymous foreign-tract inference: where a haplotype is foreign to its expectation


detect_ghost
Ghost-source detection: tracts from a population not in the panel (Plan A Workflow 3).


detect_archaic
Reference-free archaic / ghost detection via a depth-emission HMM (Plan B).


output.loo_posterior_table
Per-sample leave-one-out ancestry posterior as contiguous segments.


introgression.ReferenceQC
Result of :func:reference_qc — a per-reference admixture/mislabel audit.


introgression.GhostResult
Result of :func:detect_ghost — per-sample ghost tracts + genome-wide burden.


introgression.foreignness_track
Per-sample foreignness components as contiguous segments covering [0, L).


archaic.ArchaicResult
Result of :func:detect_archaic.






Accuracy, calibration, and fragmentation / tract-length metrics for simulated-truth experiments (the tspaint.metrics namespace).



validate.balanced_accuracy
Mean of per-true-class painting accuracies.


validate.per_base_accuracy
Span-weighted per-base painting accuracy.


validate.mean_confidence
Span-weighted mean painter confidence \|2*P(state) - 1\|.


validate.reliability_curve
Calibration curve of P(state): predicted vs. empirical per bin.


validate.breakpoint_precision_recall
Precision/recall of inferred ancestry switch points vs. truth.


validate.switch_density
Ancestry switches per unit length.


validate.tract_boundary_error
Tract-boundary localization error against the truth.


validate.breakpoint_flicker
Posterior discontinuity across a sample’s segment boundaries (CLAUDE.md §7.3).


validate.map_truth
Remap truth tracts from population ids to ancestry-state indices.






Painters scored on equal footing by a uniform harness — the full method, a runnable ARG-native baseline, and the RFMix incumbent (the tspaint.compare namespace).



compare.tspaint_paint
Paint queries with the full tspaint method (EM fit + down-pass posterior).


compare.nearest_reference_paint
Paint queries by their nearest labelled reference (ARG-native baseline).


compare.rfmix_paint
RFMix painter with the standard painter(ts, labels, queries) signature.


compare.head_to_head
Score a panel of painters on one admixture scenario across ARG substrates.


compare.score_painter
Run a painter on ts and score it against the truth.






Obtain a tspaint-ready tree sequence from genotypes (the tspaint.io namespace). The inference front ends tsinfer and singer take the same source — a tree sequence, a VCF Zarr, or a VCF; relate converts Relate .anc / .mut output. (The pre-unification names infer_tree_sequence / singer_tree_sequences / convert_relate remain as deprecated aliases.)



io.tsinfer
Infer a tree sequence from genotypes via tsinfer (CLAUDE.md §5, §9).


io.relate
Convert Relate output (.anc / .mut) to a tskit tree sequence (CLAUDE.md §5).


io.singer
Sample posterior ARGs from genotypes via SINGER (CLAUDE.md §7.4).


io.add_mutations
Overlay biallelic mutations on a tree sequence so the inference front ends have variant


io.write_haploid_vcf
Write one haploid VCF column per sample node.


io.check_persistence
Run the §5.1 persistence go/no-go on an already-loaded tree sequence.


io_genotypes.Variants
A tool-agnostic biallelic variant matrix (one column per haplotype).






The §7 horizontal coupling — a genome-axis forward-backward over each tip’s per-tree beliefs that suppresses spurious switches on inferred ARGs (the tspaint.bp namespace; also paint(smooth=True)).



bp.bp_paint
tspaint painter with horizontal BP smoothing.


bp.bp_smooth
Forward-backward over per-segment beliefs along the genome.


bp.bp_smooth_track
Smooth one tip’s :class:~tspaint.output.Segment track along the genome.


bp.bp_vs_deadband_experiment
Compare BP smoothing vs the deadband for segmentation, over seeds.






An optional, separate deliverable from painting: a time-inhomogeneous directional mugration EM that estimates the cross-ancestry transition rates q_AB(t), q_BA(t) as functions of (backward) time — locating divergence / gene-flow epochs and their direction (the tspaint.dating namespace; also Painting.rate_through_time()).



fit_rate_through_time
Fit the time-inhomogeneous directional admixture-rate-through-time profile by EM.


RateThroughTime
Result of :func:fit_rate_through_time — the directional cross-ancestry rate profile.


dating.paint_qt
Paint focal tips under a time-inhomogeneous generator Q_of_cell — the same


dating.rate_through_time_binned
Stage-1 admixture-rate-through-time profile: fit a homogeneous Q, then bin the


dating.make_Q_of_cell
Build a cell_index -> (2,2) generator callable from per-cell directional rates.


dating.directional_rate_splines
Fit q_AB(t) and q_BA(t) by GCV-selected penalised Poisson splines.


dating.fit_poisson_spline
Penalised Poisson log-spline via penalised IRLS.


dating.select_lambda_gcv
Fit at a grid of smoothing parameters; pick the one minimising GCV.


dating.log_time_grid
Geometric (log-spaced) cell edges spanning [t_min, t_max].


dating.split_branch
Split a branch [t_c, t_p] (t_c < t_p) into per-cell sub-intervals.


dating.branch_cell_stats
Per-cell endpoint-conditioned expected dwell and directional jumps for one branch.


dating.composite_transition
Composite parent→child transition ∏ expm(Q_k·d_k) for a branch under a






msprime admixture scenarios with census-event ground truth — the primary validation track and the source of examples.



simulate_admixture
Simulate an admixed sample plus two reference panels.


local_ancestry_truth
True local ancestry per sample as source-population tracts.


sim.admixture_demography
Build a two-source admixture demography with a post-pulse census.


sim.simulate_admixture_impure_refs
Simulate admixed queries, a pure reference anchor core, and impure reference panels.


sim.simulate_admixture_with_ghost
Simulate admixed queries + A/B reference panels, with an unsampled ghost source.


sim.admixture_demography_with_ghost
Two-source admixture with a third unsampled (“ghost”) source C.






The ancestry CTMC generator, transition matrices, stationary distribution, and tip emission model.



make_generator_2state
Build the 2-state ancestry CTMC generator (CLAUDE.md §2.1).


model.transition_matrix
Branch transition probabilities P(t) = expm(Q t) (CLAUDE.md §2.1).


model.stationary_distribution
Stationary distribution π of a CTMC generator.


model.tip_emission
Soft-clamp emission for a labelled tip (CLAUDE.md §2.2).


model.query_emission
Flat / root-frequency emission for an unlabelled query tip (CLAUDE.md §2.2).


model.validate_generator
Check that Q is a valid CTMC generator.






End-to-end drivers used to produce the validation results (the tspaint.experiments namespace).



experiments.admixture_experiment
Simulate admixture, fit hard-clamp EM on the references, paint the queries, score.


experiments.age_sweep
Accuracy vs. admixture age — the §9 headline.


experiments.scaling_sweep
Runtime + correctness + flicker vs. sample size.


experiments.fragmentation_experiment
Fragmentation / tract-length fidelity of hard segmentations vs. the true tracts.


experiments.arg_ensemble_experiment
Merge LAI across an ensemble of inferred ARGs vs. a single inferred ARG.


experiments.singer_ensemble_experiment
Merge LAI across SINGER posterior ARG samples vs. a single sample vs. truth.


experiments.impure_reference_experiment
Hard-clamp vs strong-Beta-soft credibility for slightly impure references.


experiments.impure_reference_sweep
Sweep :func:impure_reference_experiment across regimes (and seeds); tabulate the


experiments.flicker_vs_true_boundaries
Split a sample’s segment-boundary flicker into at-true vs. off-true boundaries.






Marginalise ARG uncertainty by averaging per-position paintings across a posterior ensemble (e.g. SINGER samples).



ensemble.merge_posterior_tables
Average per-haplotype posteriors across an ensemble of paintings.


ensemble.MergedSegment
One span of an ensemble-merged painting.






The pieces underneath the EM: per-root pruning, edge-blocked sufficient statistics, per-branch Van Loan reward integrals, the closed-form M-step, and persistence diagnostics.



pruning.prune_tree
Prune every root of a marginal tree and aggregate the results.


pruning.prune_root
Up/down sum-product pass for a single root of a marginal tree.


accumulate.accumulate_sufficient_statistics
Single E-step sweep over the tree sequence (CLAUDE.md §3.3).


branch_stats.branch_expected_stats
Endpoint-conditioned expected dwell times and jump counts on one branch.


em.build_emissions
Build the per-sample Felsenstein emission vectors (CLAUDE.md §2.2).


em.m_step_Q
Closed-form generator MLE from expected dwell times and jump counts.


em.m_step_pi
Closed-form root-frequency MLE: π = S_root / Σ S_root (CLAUDE.md §3.4).


em.m_step_w
Per-tip credibility MAP under a Beta(alpha, beta) prior (CLAUDE.md §3.4).


ranked.ranked_tree_sequence
Copy of ts with node times replaced by the dense rank of each time.

Number of distinct marginal trees in which each (internal) node appears.

Summary of the tree-sequence edge-span distribution.

title: Function reference
text:
The high-level entry point: fit the ancestry CTMC on the reference tips and paint the query haplotypes. paint returns a Painting; fit exposes the EM directly.



paint
Infer soft local ancestry along query haplotypes from a tree sequence.


Painting
Result of :func:paint: the soft local-ancestry posteriors plus the fitted model.


fit
Blocked EM for (Q, π, {w_i}) (CLAUDE.md §3, §11.1.5-6).


FitResult
Fitted parameters returned by :func:fit.

title: Function reference
text:
The soft per-haplotype posterior (Segment tracks) and its collapse into hard ancestry tracts for downstream tract-length / admixture-dating analysis.



output.posterior_table
Per-sample ancestry posterior as contiguous segments covering [0, L).


output.hard_segments
Collapse a soft posterior track into hard ancestry segments.


output.Segment
A contiguous span carrying one soft ancestry posterior.


output.missing_info_mask
Per-sample spans where the tree is uninformative (isolated sample).


output.posterior_at
Posterior vector for sample at a genomic position.

title: Function reference
text:
Audit a reference panel for admixture / mislabelling, flag foreign tracts of any haplotype without naming a donor, detect introgression from an unsampled (“ghost”) source, and — with no reference at all — detect deep archaic tracts (the tspaint.introgression and tspaint.archaic namespaces). All read the leave-one-out map, which surfaces a sample’s own foreign tracts the down-pass posterior hides; detect_ghost and detect_archaic return objects with a shared .burden / .tracts().



reference_qc
Audit a reference panel for admixture / mislabelling (Plan A Workflow 1; CLAUDE.md §9).


foreign_tracts
Anonymous foreign-tract inference: where a haplotype is foreign to its expectation


detect_ghost
Ghost-source detection: tracts from a population not in the panel (Plan A Workflow 3).


detect_archaic
Reference-free archaic / ghost detection via a depth-emission HMM (Plan B).


output.loo_posterior_table
Per-sample leave-one-out ancestry posterior as contiguous segments.


introgression.ReferenceQC
Result of :func:reference_qc — a per-reference admixture/mislabel audit.


introgression.GhostResult
Result of :func:detect_ghost — per-sample ghost tracts + genome-wide burden.


introgression.foreignness_track
Per-sample foreignness components as contiguous segments covering [0, L).


archaic.ArchaicResult
Result of :func:detect_archaic.

title: Function reference
text:
Accuracy, calibration, and fragmentation / tract-length metrics for simulated-truth experiments (the tspaint.metrics namespace).



validate.balanced_accuracy
Mean of per-true-class painting accuracies.


validate.per_base_accuracy
Span-weighted per-base painting accuracy.


validate.mean_confidence
Span-weighted mean painter confidence \|2*P(state) - 1\|.


validate.reliability_curve
Calibration curve of P(state): predicted vs. empirical per bin.


validate.breakpoint_precision_recall
Precision/recall of inferred ancestry switch points vs. truth.


validate.switch_density
Ancestry switches per unit length.


validate.tract_boundary_error

validate.breakpoint_flicker

title: Function reference
text:

title: Function reference
text:
Obtain a tspaint-ready tree sequence from genotypes (the tspaint.io namespace). The inference front ends tsinfer and singer take the same source — a tree sequence, a VCF Zarr, or a VCF; relate converts Relate .anc / .mut output. (The pre-unification names infer_tree_sequence / singer_tree_sequences / convert_relate remain as deprecated aliases.)

io.relate

Overlay biallelic mutations on a tree sequence so the inference front ends have variant

title: Function reference
text:
The §7 horizontal coupling — a genome-axis forward-backward over each tip’s per-tree beliefs that suppresses spurious switches on inferred ARGs (the tspaint.bp namespace; also paint(smooth=True)).

title: Function reference

An optional, separate deliverable from painting: a time-inhomogeneous directional mugration EM that estimates the cross-ancestry transition rates q_AB(t), q_BA(t) as functions of (backward) time — locating divergence / gene-flow epochs and their direction (the tspaint.dating namespace; also Painting.rate_through_time()).

Simulate admixed queries + A/B reference panels, with an unsampled ghost source.

title: output.hard_segments
text:
tspaint.output.hard_segments(track, deadband=0.0)
Collapse a soft posterior track into hard ancestry segments.
Produces [(left, right, state), ...] — the object a downstream tract-length / admixture-pulse dating analysis consumes.



track : list[Segment]

Soft posterior segments for one sample (e.g. from :func:posterior_table).

deadband : float = 0.0

Confidence dead-band on the top-two posterior margin max(P) - 2nd-max(P). A switch to a new argmax state is accepted only where margin >= deadband; otherwise the previous state is carried forward. Default 0.0 (naive argmax).






 : list[tuple[float, float, int]]

Hard (left, right, state) ancestry segments.





A positive deadband suppresses the low-confidence (~P=0.5) flips that fragment long tracts under naive argmax (deadband=0). Because the posterior is calibrated, a modest dead-band (≈0.3–0.5) recovers the true switch density and tract-length distribution where naive argmax over-fragments ~3× (CLAUDE.md §9). This is a tunable precision/recall dial a fixed hard segmenter (e.g. RFMix’s CRF) does not expose. MISSING_INFO spans carry the previous state forward; the first interval takes its argmax.

Because the posterior is calibrated, a modest dead-band (≈0.3–0.5) recovers the true switch density and tract-length distribution where naive argmax over-fragments ~3× (CLAUDE.md §9). title: Overview
text:
tspaint infers soft, calibrated local ancestry along individual haplotypes from an inferred tree sequence (Relate → tskit, or tsinfer/SINGER). Ancestry is modelled as a discrete character evolving up the branches of each marginal tree under a continuous-time Markov chain (a CTMC “mugration” model); reference haplotypes label tips through a soft noise model with learned per-tip credibility; and the parameters are fit by EM whose sufficient statistics are accumulated per tree-sequence edge, span-weighted — so a clade persisting across many trees is counted once. The output is, for every haplotype at every position, a calibrated posterior P(ancestry) — soft, not a hard call.
A separate, optional path — admixture dating — reuses the same engine with a time-inhomogeneous generator to estimate when the labelled ancestries diverged or exchanged genes, as directional cross-ancestry rates q_AB(t), q_BA(t) through time. It is its own deliverable and does not change the painting.
See the model for the generative construction, inference for the EM math, admixture dating for the rate-through-time estimator, and assumptions for the sharp edges.

title: Overview
text:
Install
pixi install          # dev environment (tskit, msprime, numpy, scipy, …)
pixi run test         # run the test suite

title: Overview
text:
Quickstart
import tspaint

# 1. Get a tree sequence — simulate admixture with known truth,
#    or build one from genotypes: tspaint.io.infer_tree_sequence / singer_tree_sequences.
ts = tspaint.simulate_admixture(n_admix=10, n_ref=10, sequence_length=2e6,
                              T_admix=100, T_split=5000, Ne=1000, random_seed=1)

# 2. Label the reference haplotypes (sample-node id -> ancestry state); paint the rest.
labels = {...}                              # source-A tips -> 0, source-B tips -> 1
painting = tspaint.paint(ts, labels)          # EM-fit on references, paint the queries

painting.posteriors[q]                      # soft per-position posterior (Segments)
painting.segments(deadband=0.4)             # hard ancestry tracts (tract-length distribution)

# 3. Optional: admixture dating — a separate deliverable, reusing the painting's fit.
rtt = painting.rate_through_time()          # or tspaint.fit_rate_through_time(ts, labels)
rtt.plot()                                  # q_AB(t), q_BA(t): when the ancestries diverged / mixed
On an inferred (tsinfer / Relate) ARG, add smooth=True to suppress tree-inference-induced spurious switches with the horizontal BP smoother.
Two routes lead to admixture timing: the tract-length distribution from painting.segments(...) (an indirect, downstream input to pulse-dating methods), and the direct rate-through-time estimator fit_rate_through_time, which fits the cross-ancestry rate as a function of time.

title: Overview
text:
The deliverable
For each query haplotype, the soft posterior over ancestry at every position (red = ancestry A, blue = B, white where the local tree cannot tell), with the true ancestry shown beneath each row:

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
This page is the honest accounting of what tspaint assumes, where its parameters can fail to be identified, and the regimes in which it stops working. It is the companion to the Overview: the Overview tells you how to run the method; this page tells you when to trust the result and when not to. None of the caveats below are hypothetical hedges — each is either forced by the model or has been measured directly. The MEASURED numbers are quoted verbatim from the project’s validation runs.
The short version: tspaint is a generative model — a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) for an ancestry character riding up the branches of an inferred tree sequence, fitted by EM. Generative models give you calibrated soft posteriors and an interpretable, readable Q, but they also have identifiability structure that a black-box classifier hides. The sharp edges here are the price of that interpretability.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
1. Input requirement: cross-tree node-ID stability






WarningThe premise is delivered by the input, not the method



tspaint accumulates its sufficient statistics per tree-sequence edge, span-weighted (the lead novelty, CLAUDE.md §3.3). This is what removes double-counting and is the channel through which genome-scale autocorrelation enters: a clade that persists across many marginal trees must be one set of edges with a wide span, identified by a stable node ID across those trees. If the input does not deliver that stability, the central premise is not met — the method is still correct, but it is no better than independent per-tree pruning.


Two front ends deliver the persistence invariant:

Relate → tskit with --compress. The --compress flag (Nathaniel S. Pope) assigns the same age to nodes with identical descendant sets across adjacent trees, unifying a persistent subclade into a single long-span node. This is not optional. Without it, Relate’s conversion can mint fresh node IDs per local tree even for unchanged descendant sets, producing short churning edges that retain the double-counting fix but lose almost all autocorrelation benefit.
tsinfer / tsdate tree sequences have native cross-tree node-ID stability by construction and are an equally valid (or preferable) front end.

Two source-confirmed caveats on --compress (Speidel et al., 2019; relate_lib):

It unifies on exact sorted-leaf-set equality, no threshold (tree_sequence.hpp::FindIdenticalNodes) — provably stricter than Relate’s own “equivalent branch” notion (Pearson correlation ≥ 0.9). A clade that persists but gains or loses a single tip at a breakpoint is split into separate node IDs. Autocorrelation capture is therefore slightly conservative (the safe direction: it never falsely merges distinct lineages, only under-links near-equivalent ones).
The global root is deliberately re-IDed whenever either of its children changes (to avoid forcing a genome-wide constant TMRCA), and merged node ages are span-weighted-averaged then monotonicity-constrained. We skip root branches anyway (see §4), so the first is benign; the second compounds the time-calibration concern of §3.


1.1 The persistence go/no-go
Before any inference, run the persistence check (CLAUDE.md §5.1). It is the go/no-go test on the method’s premise — the histogram of how many distinct marginal trees each internal node survives:
import tskit, numpy as np
from collections import Counter

ts = tskit.load("example.trees")

# how many distinct trees each internal node survives <-- THE key histogram
node_tree_count = Counter()
for (_interval, eout, ein), tree in zip(ts.edge_diffs(), ts.trees()):
    for u in tree.nodes():
        if not tree.is_sample(u):
            node_tree_count[u] += 1
counts = np.array(list(node_tree_count.values()))
print("internal-node persistence: median", np.median(counts),
      "max", counts.max(), "frac==1", np.mean(counts == 1))

If frac==1 is near 1 (persistence spiked at a single tree), --compress did not take or something upstream is wrong — the method’s premise is not met. Stop and fix the input.
If there is real mass above 1, the horizontal coupling is getting signal. Proceed.

The packaged helper diagnostics.persistence_summary computes the same histogram.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
2. Identifiability & collapse modes
The model is well-pinned with a hard-clamped reference panel. The identifiability problems appear only once labels are allowed to move — i.e. once per-tip credibility \(w_i\) is learned (the soft-reference / introgression-mapping mode, CLAUDE.md §2.2). The danger is structural: when a reference’s genealogical context disagrees with its label, the model has three ways to explain the same disagreement, and they trade off against each other.









Collapse mode
What the model does
Why it is degenerate
Guard




Lower \(w_i\)
Declares this haplotype admixed and stops trusting its label
Any disagreement can be absorbed tip-by-tip; the panel dissolves
Hard-clamped anchor set (\(w_i \equiv 1\)); only the rest float


Inflate \(Q\)
Says everyone switches ancestry fast
Large \(Q\cdot t\) washes every branch toward \(\pi\); tips stop informing their ancestors
Informative \(\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)\) prior near 1 keeps labels believed


Relabel a clade
Flips the ancestry assignment of a whole subtree
Pure label-switching: the likelihood is invariant under a global relabel
Never let the whole panel go soft simultaneously



The implementation enforces the anchor guard directly: fit raises ValueError if soft_refs covers every labelled tip, with the message “keep a hard-clamped anchor set; never let the whole panel float (CLAUDE.md §6)”.






NoteTwo degenerate fixed points to watch in tests




\(Q \to 0\) freezes the initialization: with no migration the up-pass never mixes states, so whatever the tips say propagates unchanged and EM never moves off its starting point.
\(Q \to \infty\) washes everything to \(\pi\): every branch becomes the stationary distribution regardless of its tips, so all posteriors relax to the prior and all discriminating signal is lost.

Both are exercised by the degenerate-case tests (test_em_degenerate.py). They are also the two ends of the \(Q\)-inflation collapse above.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
3. \(\pi\)-identifiability — the measured real failure mode






WarningWhen deep branches wash, \(\pi\) drifts and the painting collapses to one colour — confidently



This is not a hypothetical. It is the failure mode that the order-only ablation (§3.1) surfaced and that the head-to-head harness caught on short genomes. Hold \(\pi\) fixed.


The root frequencies \(\pi\) are a prior on the state of the genealogical MRCA. The M-step estimate is
\[
\pi = \frac{S_{\text{root}}}{\sum_s S_{\text{root}}[s]}
\tag{1}\]
where \(S_{\text{root}}\) is the span-weighted expected root-state mass pooled over all roots (m_step_pi). The problem: when the deep/root branches wash — large \(Q\cdot t\), as happens on sparse or short ARGs, or under the order-only variant — each tree’s root marginal just echoes \(\pi\) rather than carrying independent evidence about it. So Equation 1 feeds \(\pi\) back onto itself, \(\pi\) is under-determined, and the M-step drifts it to a degenerate extreme (e.g. \(\pi \approx [0.96, 0.04]\)). With one state dominating the prior, every query relaxes toward that state, and the painting collapses to one colour with high confidence — confidently wrong.
The fix: hold \(\pi\) fixed (estimate_pi=False, the tspaint_paint default). Uniform \(\pi\) is principled, not a kludge: \(\pi\) is a prior on the arbitrary GMRCA state, so uniform is the right non-informative choice. It is estimating \(\pi\) from washing roots that breaks. The lower-level fit exposes estimate_pi=True only for deliberate \(\pi\)-recovery studies, where deep branches are well-resolved and \(\pi\) is identified.
MEASURED recovery numbers (CLAUDE.md §6, §9):



Regime
estimate_pi=True (balanced acc.)
estimate_pi=False




true ARG, order-only (ranked)
0.50
0.94


tsinfer, \(L = 5\times10^4\)
0.50
1.00


tsinfer, \(L = 2\times10^5\) (good data)
0.984
0.999



The fix recovers both failures and is harmless on good, long data (the \(L=2\times10^5\) row, where \(\pi\) is identified). The short/sparse regime is itself an edge case — real LAI runs on long genomes with many roots, where \(\pi\) is identified and tspaint already paints 0.98–1.0 — so holding \(\pi\) fixed is a free robustness default, not a patch over a practical limitation.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
4. Branch-length / time-calibration risk
The CTMC rides on branch lengths through \(P(t) = \exp(Q\,t)\), so \(Q\) depends on calibrated node times. Relate estimates branch lengths under a panmictic coalescent prior — misspecified precisely in the structured / admixed regime that LAI targets (Speidel et al., 2019, Supp. Fig. 3c: TMRCA bias under the wrong \(N_e\)). If \(Q\) depends on those biased \(t\), the bias propagates. --compress’s span-weighted-average-then-reconcile age smoothing (§1) adds a further calibration layer on top.

3.1 The order-only ablation — a measured negative result






WarningThe hypothesised fix makes things worse. Do not use it for inference.



The order-only / ranked-topology variant (ranked_tree_sequence) was the natural robustness fix for the calibration risk — discard the (possibly biased) time magnitudes, keep only coalescence order, in the spirit of Relate’s order-based selection test. It does not work. It is kept as a runnable ablation only, never a recommended mode.


The variant replaces each node’s absolute time with the dense rank of that time (order preserved, magnitudes discarded; topology and sample IDs unchanged, so labels and truth transfer). MEASURED: it collapses true-ARG painting from ~1.0 to ~0.50 (CLAUDE.md §6). The mechanism is a chain:

Dense-ranking compresses the timescale.
EM compensates by fitting a much larger \(Q\) (~\(10^{-2}\) vs. ~\(5\times10^{-5}\)).
The large \(Q\) washes the deep/root branches.
Washed roots make \(\pi\) unidentifiable — it drifts to a degenerate extreme (§3) → confident-wrong painting.

So the §4 worry (mis-calibration hurts) was correct, but this particular cure is wrong: it discards exactly the coalescence-depth magnitudes the CTMC needs, and it worsens the \(\pi\) degeneracy. The actual robustness fix for the same \(\pi\) failure is estimate_pi=False (§3), not order-only.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
5. The mugration approximation






WarningGenealogy and ancestry are jointly distributed; tspaint treats the genealogy as fixed



tspaint models ancestry as a discrete character evolving on a fixed genealogy (the “mugration” / discrete-phylogeography construction, Lemey et al., 2009). This ignores that the genealogy and the ancestry are not independent: under the structured coalescent, lineages in different demes cannot coalesce, so the tree shape itself carries ancestry information that the mugration model throws away. The consequence is a bias in the estimated \(Q\).


This is a deliberate v1 simplification, stated openly. The structured-coalescent methods that correct it — BASTA (De Maio et al., 2015), MASCOT (Müller et al., 2018), and, on inferred ARGs specifically, SCAR (Guo et al., 2022) — solve the joint genealogy↔︎ancestry problem, but their deliverable is migration demography (rates, \(N_e\), ancestral lineage locations), not per-haplotype painting with credibility. For a v1 painting tool the naive mugration version is likely fine in practice; the limitation is that the learned \(Q\) should be read as an effective ancestral-migration timescale, not an unbiased structured-coalescent rate.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
section: 6. Loss of signal at old admixture
text:
6. Loss of signal at old admixture






NoteA coalescent limit, not a tree-inference one



At old admixture under present-day sampling, accuracy decays even on the true ARG. This is not tree-inference error — it is the genealogy itself losing the information.


The headline hypothesis is that a tree-native method can reach older admixture than tract-/copying-based methods (RFMix, MOSAIC, FLARE), because the genealogical relationship of a query to its source references survives even after recombination shreds the surrounding haplotype. The binding constraint becomes tree accuracy rather than tract length. That is largely borne out — but with a measured ceiling.
MEASURED, discrimination vs. admixture age on a true ARG (deep split so only tract length varies; balanced 50/50 admixture; balanced accuracy / mean confidence; CLAUDE.md §9):








\(T_{\text{admix}}\) (generations)
balanced accuracy
mean confidence




30
0.99
0.72


300
0.93
0.36


1000
0.50
0.15


3000
0.50
0.18



tspaint discriminates well at recent-to-moderate admixture and loses the reference signal at old admixture. Crucially, at \(T_{\text{admix}} = 1000\) this is not tree-inference error (the ARG is true) but coalescent structure: admixed individuals sampled at the present coalesce among themselves before the old pulse, which severs the query→reference genealogical link the method depends on. This refines — does not retract — the headline: tract length is not the binding constraint, but the query↔︎reference link is, and that link decays with admixture age under present-day admixed sampling. The crossover age is scenario-specific (it scales with \(N_e\) and the sampling scheme); the qualitative loss-at-old is robust.






NoteReporting note: report balanced accuracy + confidence, not plain accuracy



On a lopsided truth, plain accuracy is misleading — taking argmax of a posterior near \(P=0.5\) can score high while carrying no real information. Use validate.balanced_accuracy together with validate.mean_confidence, as the tables above do.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
section: 7. Inherited from the front end: ARG accuracy is the binding constraint
text:
7. Inherited from the front end: ARG accuracy is the binding constraint
Even setting aside old-admixture coalescent limits, painting on an inferred ARG is bounded by the ARG’s quality. MEASURED on a tsinfer-inferred ARG (balanced 50/50, chance 0.5; CLAUDE.md §9): per-base accuracy ~0.53 at ~650 sites (poor ARG, near chance), ~0.64 at ~2100 sites, ~0.88 at ~5400 sites (good ARG). Tree accuracy, not tract length, is the binding constraint — exactly as predicted. The practical implication: feed tspaint the best ARG you can. A Bayesian SMC sampler such as SINGER (io.singer_tree_sequences) largely lifts this bound, and averaging across a posterior ensemble (ensemble.merge_posterior_tables) turns the residual ARG uncertainty into a calibrated band rather than a hidden error.

title: Assumptions, identifiability & limitations
text:
Summary



Concern
Status
Mitigation / default




Cross-tree node-ID stability
Required premise
--compress or tsinfer; run the persistence check first


Label-switching / \(w_i\) / \(Q\) collapse
Structural (soft mode)
Hard-clamped anchor set; \(\mathrm{Beta}\) prior near 1; never float the whole panel


\(\pi\) drift on washed roots
Measured failure
estimate_pi=False (default); uniform \(\pi\) is principled


Panmictic branch-length bias
Real; biases \(Q\)
Order-only ablation does not help (measured); feed well-calibrated times (SINGER)


Mugration approximation
Deliberate v1 simplification
Read \(Q\) as an effective timescale; BASTA/MASCOT/SCAR correct it


Old admixture under present sampling
Coalescent limit
None — report balanced accuracy + confidence honestly


Inferred-ARG quality
Binding constraint
Best available ARG; SINGER posterior + ensemble merge



The throughline: tspaint’s honesty is its selling point relative to black-box ARG-native classifiers. It produces a calibrated soft posterior, a readable \(Q\), and explicit per-tip credibility — and, in the regimes where the signal is gone, it relaxes toward \(\pi\) rather than making a confident wrong call.

title: Code organization and data flow
text: This page is the map a reader or contributor needs to navigate the tspaint source: how the modules fit together, what flows between them, and which invariants keep the result correct. It documents the code paths, not the science — the authoritative model and its justification live in CLAUDE.md (referenced below as §N). Names here are verified against src/tspaint/__init__.py; links go to the API reference where one exists.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
The one call
The whole library funnels through a single high-level entry point:
import tspaint

ts     = tspaint.simulate_admixture(n_admix=10, n_ref=10)   # or tspaint.io.infer_tree_sequence(...)
labels = {0: 0, 1: 0, 2: 1, 3: 1}                         # reference sample-node -> ancestry state
painting = tspaint.paint(ts, labels)                        # EM-fit on references, paint the queries

painting.posteriors[q]            # soft per-position posterior for query q (list[Segment])
painting.segments(deadband=0.4)   # hard ancestry tracts for downstream dating
paint ties three stages together — fit, paint, segment — and returns a Painting. Everything else in the package is either machinery underneath those stages or tooling around them (front ends, comparators, metrics, experiment drivers).

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
End-to-end data flow

flowchart LR
  subgraph IN["Input front ends — tspaint.io"]
    TSI["io_tsinfer<br/>infer_tree_sequence"]
    SNG["io_singer<br/>singer_tree_sequences"]
    REL["Relate --compress<br/>(external)"]
  end

  TS["tskit.TreeSequence<br/>(cross-tree node-ID stable)"]
  LBL["labels: dict<br/>ref node -> state"]

  TSI --> TS
  SNG --> TS
  REL --> TS
  TS --> PAINT
  LBL --> PAINT

  subgraph PAINT["paint() — api.py"]
    direction TB

    subgraph FIT["fit() — em.py  (blocked EM loop)"]
      direction TB
      EMIT["build_emissions<br/>soft clamp + query"]
      ACC["accumulate.py<br/>edge_diffs sweep,<br/>bank per edge × span"]
      PRU["pruning.py<br/>up/down per root<br/>(γ, ξ, loo, loglik)"]
      VL["branch_stats.py<br/>Van Loan rewards<br/>(dwell, jumps)"]
      MSTEP["M-step (em.py)<br/>m_step_Q / _pi / _w"]
      EMIT --> ACC
      ACC --> PRU
      ACC --> VL
      PRU --> ACC
      VL --> ACC
      ACC --> MSTEP
      MSTEP -->|next iter| EMIT
    end

    POST["posterior_table — output.py<br/>down-pass γ per query<br/>-> Segment tracks"]
    SMOOTH["bp/horizontal.py<br/>(optional smooth=True)"]

    FIT -->|"Q, π, w"| POST
    POST --> SMOOTH
  end

  PAINT --> PAINTING["Painting<br/>.posteriors (soft)"]
  PAINTING --> SEG[".segments(deadband)<br/>output.hard_segments<br/>(hard tracts)"]
  PAINTING --> METRICS["tspaint.metrics / tspaint.compare<br/>(scoring vs. truth)"]




flowchart LR
  subgraph IN["Input front ends — tspaint.io"]
    TSI["io_tsinfer<br/>infer_tree_sequence"]
    SNG["io_singer<br/>singer_tree_sequences"]
    REL["Relate --compress<br/>(external)"]
  end

  TS["tskit.TreeSequence<br/>(cross-tree node-ID stable)"]
  LBL["labels: dict<br/>ref node -> state"]

  TSI --> TS
  SNG --> TS
  REL --> TS
  TS --> PAINT
  LBL --> PAINT

  subgraph PAINT["paint() — api.py"]
    direction TB

    subgraph FIT["fit() — em.py  (blocked EM loop)"]
      direction TB
      EMIT["build_emissions<br/>soft clamp + query"]
      ACC["accumulate.py<br/>edge_diffs sweep,<br/>bank per edge × span"]
      PRU["pruning.py<br/>up/down per root<br/>(γ, ξ, loo, loglik)"]
      VL["branch_stats.py<br/>Van Loan rewards<br/>(dwell, jumps)"]
      MSTEP["M-step (em.py)<br/>m_step_Q / _pi / _w"]
      EMIT --> ACC
      ACC --> PRU
      ACC --> VL
      PRU --> ACC
      VL --> ACC
      ACC --> MSTEP
      MSTEP -->|next iter| EMIT
    end

    POST["posterior_table — output.py<br/>down-pass γ per query<br/>-> Segment tracks"]
    SMOOTH["bp/horizontal.py<br/>(optional smooth=True)"]

    FIT -->|"Q, π, w"| POST
    POST --> SMOOTH
  end

  PAINT --> PAINTING["Painting<br/>.posteriors (soft)"]
  PAINTING --> SEG[".segments(deadband)<br/>output.hard_segments<br/>(hard tracts)"]
  PAINTING --> METRICS["tspaint.metrics / tspaint.compare<br/>(scoring vs. truth)"]







Walkthrough
0. Get a tree sequence. tspaint paints any tskit.TreeSequence whose nodes keep stable IDs across marginal trees (§5). The tspaint.io namespace gathers the supported ways to obtain one from genotypes; a true/simulated ARG works directly.
1. Emissions (§2.2). build_emissions (in em.py) turns the labels into per-sample Felsenstein emission vectors. A labelled reference tip i gets the soft clamp w_i · 1[label] + (1 − w_i) · π (anchors default to w = 1, a hard clamp); every other sample gets the query emission π (flat / root-frequency). This is rebuilt each EM iteration because w and π move.
2. E-step: pruning, per tree, per root (§3.1). pruning.py runs exact two-pass sum-product message passing on each marginal tree. The up-pass (post-order) accumulates partial likelihoods L_u(s) as a product over all children (polytomy-safe); the down-pass (pre-order) yields the posterior marginal γ_u, the joint parent–child posterior ξ_{(p,c)} (a K×K object per branch), a leave-one-out marginal loo_u (the outside message excluding a tip’s own emission — credibility evidence that cannot self-confirm), and the exact tree log-likelihood. A marginal tree may be a forest, so this runs once per root and aggregates.
3. Edge-blocked, span-weighted sufficient statistics (§3.3 — the correctness core). accumulate.py drives the genome with zip(ts.edge_diffs(), ts.trees()). For each marginal tree it prunes once, then banks each entering edge’s contribution exactly once, on entry, weighted by that edge’s own span (right − left). A clade persisting across many trees is one set of wide-span edges, so summing by span partitions the genome without double-counting (the edge-table invariant: the intervals on which a node is a child are disjoint). Root-state mass is accumulated per interval, not per edge. The accumulators are S_dwell, S_jumps, S_root, per-tip S_cred (agree/disagree for the credibility update), and a span-integrated loglik.
4. Per-branch Van Loan rewards (§3.2). For each entering edge, branch_stats.py computes the endpoint-conditioned expected dwell time per state and expected jump counts per ordered pair via the Van Loan block-triangular matrix exponential (Van Loan, 1978; Hobolth and Jensen, 2011). The (Q, t)-dependent part (branch_kernel) is cached by branch length and applied to each edge’s ξ cheaply (stats_from_kernel), so the expensive expm runs once per distinct branch length per iteration, not once per edge. This module is the designated Phasic seam (§12): swap the expm blocks for Phasic without changing callers.
5. M-step (closed form, §3.4). Back in em.py, the pooled statistics give the generator m_step_Q (q_mn = S_jumps[m,n] / S_dwell[m]), the root frequencies m_step_pi, and the per-tip credibility m_step_w (MAP under a Beta(α, β) prior). fit iterates steps 1–5 to a log-likelihood tolerance or max_iter, returning a FitResult (Q, π, {w_i}, loglik_history). The default estimate_pi=False holds π uniform — the robust choice, since π is a prior on the arbitrary GMRCA state and estimating it from washing deep branches is the §6 degeneracy.
6. Deliverable: the soft painting (§2.4). With the fitted (Q, π, w), paint rebuilds emissions and calls posterior_table (in output.py), which runs the down-pass per marginal tree and records each query’s posterior as contiguous Segments covering [0, L). Spans where a sample is an isolated root are tagged MISSING_INFO (the tree carries no information — the posterior is the prior π, distinct from a 50–50 uncertain call). The result is wrapped in a Painting.
7. Optional horizontal smoothing (§7). With paint(..., smooth=True), bp/horizontal.py runs a single-pass genome-axis forward–backward (EP approximation) over each tip’s per-tree beliefs, with a per-breakpoint near-identity transition (switch penalty epsilon). This adds the propagation of uncertainty across persist-but-reparent breakpoints that blocked EM structurally omits — recommended on inferred (tsinfer / Relate) ARGs, redundant on a true ARG.
8. Hard tracts. Painting.segments(deadband) collapses the soft posteriors into hard (left, right, state) tracts via hard_segments. The deadband suppresses low-confidence argmax flips that fragment long tracts — a precision/recall dial a fixed hard segmenter does not expose. This is the object a downstream tract-length / admixture-pulse dating analysis consumes.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
A parallel path: admixture dating (tspaint.dating)
Dating is not part of the paint pipeline — it is a separate estimator that reuses the same engine (pruning + Van Loan rewards + edge-blocked accumulation) with one change: the generator becomes time-inhomogeneous, piecewise-constant on a logarithmic time grid. The flow (admixture dating has the math):

grid (dating/grid.py) — log_time_grid / split_branch cut each branch at the cell boundaries it crosses.
E-step (dating/estep.py) — branch_cell_stats sandwiches the per-cell Van Loan reward between the composite transition products above/below it on the branch, conditioned on the same endpoint posterior ξ; accumulated per edge, span-weighted, into per-cell occupation D and directional jumps J. Summed over cells this collapses to the homogeneous branch_expected_stats (a tested invariant).
M-step (dating/mstep.py) — each direction’s rate is a penalised-Poisson log-spline with GCV smoothing, restricted to the informative occupation window (directional_rate_splines).
EM loop (dating/em.py) — fit_rate_through_time alternates the two to a monotone log-likelihood, returning a RateThroughTime (q_AB(t), q_BA(t), .plot()).

Entry points: fit_rate_through_time(ts, labels) standalone, or Painting.rate_through_time() which warm-starts from the painting’s fitted (Q, π, w) (skips the homogeneous fit) and returns a new profile — it does not modify painting.posteriors. Feeding Q(t) back into painting (dating.paint_qt) is available but off the default path: it gives no painting-accuracy gain, only sharper confidence at ~4–5× cost, so the paths stay side by side.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
Public API map
The top-level package re-exports a curated surface and a set of grouped namespaces. The submodules are all importable directly too (e.g. tspaint.pruning), but the table below is the intended entry map.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
Front ends (tspaint.io)
tspaint is front-end agnostic: it paints any tree sequence with cross-tree node-ID stability (§5). The supported routes to one:



Front end
Module
What it does




tsinfer
io_tsinfer
infer_tree_sequence runs tsinfer on genotypes → a point-estimate ARG with native node-ID stability. add_mutations overlays variants on a bare ARG first. The realistic substrate where tree accuracy becomes the binding constraint (§9).


SINGER
io_singer
singer_tree_sequences shells out to the SINGER binary (haploid VCF → singer -ploidy 1, with a retry loop → tskit), returning posterior ARG samples. These represent genuine ARG uncertainty, so they are the input to the ensemble merge (§7.4). Sample order preserved; times in generations.


Relate --compress
external (io_relate)
Convert outside Python with Convert --mode ConvertToTreeSequence --compress, then tskit.load. --compress is load-bearing — it unifies a persistent clade into one long-span node ID (§5), the invariant the edge-blocking depends on.



RFMix is not a front end — it is genotype-native and appears as a comparator (below).

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
Comparators (tspaint.compare)
A painter is any f(ts, labels, queries) -> {sample: [Segment]}. The harness scores every painter against the same simulated truth on equal footing, so tspaint, simple baselines, and external tools compare directly.



Painter
Module
What it is




tspaint_paint
compare
The full method (EM fit + down-pass posterior) in painter form.


nearest_reference_paint
compare
ARG-native baseline: paint each query by its nearest labelled reference (smallest TMRCA) per tree. No CTMC, no EM — the genealogy painter the generative model should beat.


rfmix_paint
io_rfmix
The field-standard segment incumbent (RFMix v2): writes phased VCFs, shells out to rfmix (isolated compare pixi env), parses the .fb.tsv posteriors back to Segment tracks.


head_to_head
compare
Driver: simulate one admixture scenario, score a panel of painters across ARG substrates ("true", "tsinfer").


score_painter
compare
Run one painter and return balanced accuracy, accuracy, and mean confidence.



To add a comparator (MOSAIC/FLARE, ARGMix, AncestralPaths, …), implement the same painter signature — shell out to the tool over a VCF, parse its calls into Segments — and pass it to head_to_head; it is then scored like the rest.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
Invariants that keep it correct
These come straight from the tskit data model and the method’s design (§4, §12). A naive traversal violates them and silently corrupts the sufficient statistics or the painting — they are the load-bearing rules, not optional polish.








Invariant
Where enforced
Why




Accumulate per edge, span-weighted, banked once on entry
accumulate.py (edges_in loop, w_edge = e.right − e.left)
A persistent clade is one wide-span edge set; summing by span partitions the genome without double-counting and is the channel for genome-scale autocorrelation. Never sum per-(tree × branch).


Prune per root
pruning.prune_tree (for r in tree.roots)
A marginal tree may be a forest; each root carries its own π. There may be no single root.


Skip root branches
accumulate.py / pruning.py (root has no parent edge); branch_kernel returns None for t ≤ 0
Root branch length is 0 by definition in tskit; root state enters via π (S_root) only, never via Q on a (nonexistent) root edge.


Polytomy-safe traversal
pruning.py (tree.children(u) / sib pointers; product over all children)
Internal nodes with >2 children are real and first-class; never assume binarity.


Isolated span = missing-info ≠ uncertain
pruning.py (tags missing_info), output.py (MISSING_INFO status, missing_info_mask)
An isolated sample’s posterior falls back to the prior and is tagged distinctly; conflating “no information” with “50–50” is a real interpretive error for introgression mapping.


Keep a hard-clamped anchor set
em.fit (raises ValueError if soft_refs covers every labelled tip)
Never let the whole panel float — that reintroduces unsupervised degeneracy (label switching, Q/w trade-off, collapse).


Input must have cross-tree node-ID stability
front ends (tspaint.io); diagnostics (diagnostics.node_persistence)
The edge-blocking and horizontal coupling depend on a persistent clade being one node ID across trees. Delivered natively by msprime/tsinfer/SINGER, or by Relate --compress. Verify with the persistence histogram before trusting results.


Leave-one-out for credibility
pruning.py (loo), accumulate.py (S_cred)
Judge a tip’s label against what the rest of the tree says, not its own self-confirming posterior.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
Module reference



Module
Responsibility




api.py
High-level paint pipeline and the Painting result. The one call most users need.


model.py
Ancestry CTMC: generator Q, root frequencies π, transition matrices, tip/query emissions, the soft-credibility noise model. Generator-agnostic; touches no tskit.


pruning.py
Felsenstein two-pass sum-product per marginal tree, per root, polytomy-safe. Produces γ, ξ, leave-one-out marginals, missing-info tags, exact log-likelihood. The E-step kernel.


branch_stats.py
Per-branch endpoint-conditioned expected dwell times and jump counts via the Van Loan block exponential. Length-cached. The designated Phasic seam.


accumulate.py
The correctness core: edge_diffs sweep, edge-blocked span-weighted sufficient statistics, banked once per edge.


em.py
EM orchestration (fit) + the closed-form M-step (m_step_Q/_pi/_w) + build_emissions.


output.py
The deliverable: per-haplotype, per-position posterior as Segment tracks; hard_segments collapse; missing_info_mask; posterior_at.


bp/horizontal.py
Deferred §7 horizontal coupling: single-pass genome-axis forward–backward (EP) over per-tree beliefs; bp_paint, bp_smooth, bp_smooth_track. Helps on inferred ARGs.


dating/grid.py
Log-time grid for the time-inhomogeneous estimator: log_time_grid, cell_centers, split_branch (cut a branch at cell boundaries).


dating/estep.py
Per-cell endpoint-conditioned stats (branch_cell_stats, Van Loan sandwiched by composite transitions), time-binned accumulation, and the Q(t) painter paint_qt.


dating/mstep.py
Directional penalised-Poisson log-spline rate fit with GCV smoothing: fit_poisson_spline, select_lambda_gcv, directional_rate_splines.


dating/em.py
The time-inhomogeneous EM (fit_rate_through_time) → RateThroughTime; make_Q_of_cell. The tspaint.dating headline — a parallel path to paint, not part of it.


ensemble.py
Marginalise ARG uncertainty: average per-position paintings across a posterior ensemble (e.g. SINGER) → MergedSegments with a spread band.


io_tsinfer.py
tsinfer front end: add_mutations, infer_tree_sequence.


io_singer.py
SINGER front end: haploid VCF → posterior ARG samples; write_haploid_vcf, singer_tree_sequences.


io_rfmix.py
RFMix comparator (genotype-native): VCF/map writers, run_rfmix, .fb.tsv/.msp.tsv parsers, rfmix_paint.


io_relate.py
Relate --compress → tskit wrappers/checks (external C++ toolchain).


io.py
The tspaint.io namespace — gathers the front ends.


compare.py
Head-to-head harness: painters (tspaint_paint, nearest_reference_paint, rfmix_paint), score_painter, head_to_head.


validate.py
Metrics (exposed as tspaint.metrics): accuracy, calibration, flicker, tract-boundary error, fragmentation, map_truth.


sim.py
msprime admixture sims with census-event ground truth; simulate_admixture, local_ancestry_truth, admixture_demography.


ranked.py
Order-only (dense-ranked) ablation (ranked_tree_sequence). Measured not beneficial (§6); kept runnable behind tspaint_paint(..., ranked=True).


diagnostics.py
Front-end-agnostic premise checks: node-persistence histogram and edge-span summary — the go/no-go on the central premise (§5.1).


experiments.py
End-to-end benchmark drivers (tspaint.experiments): sim → fit → paint → score, age/scaling sweeps, ensemble and SINGER drivers, the flicker metric.

title: Code organization and data flow
text:
See also

API reference — full signatures and parameter docs.
CLAUDE.md — the authoritative spec (model, math, tree-sequence accounting, validation plan), with the §N anchors referenced throughout this page.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
Painting answers what ancestry a haplotype carries at a locus. A different question is when the labelled ancestries diverged or exchanged genes. tspaint answers it with a separate, optional estimator that rides the same engine: make the ancestry CTMC time-inhomogeneous and fit the two directional cross-ancestry transition rates \(q_{AB}(t)\), \(q_{BA}(t)\) as smooth functions of (backward) time. The result — a RateThroughTime profile — locates divergence and gene-flow epochs, their direction and asymmetry, and ongoing flow.
This path lives side by side with painting. It does not change how paint works, and Q(t) is deliberately not fed back into the default painting (see Why not paint(dating=True) below). The design and its rung-by-rung validation are in notes/admix_dating_design.md.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
The model
The homogeneous painter uses one generator \(Q\) for the whole genealogy. Here \(Q\) becomes a piecewise-constant function of time on a fine logarithmic grid (logarithmic so each cell carries comparable coalescent power):
\[
Q(t) \;=\; Q_k \;=\;
\begin{pmatrix} -q_{AB}^{(k)} & q_{AB}^{(k)} \\[2pt] q_{BA}^{(k)} & -q_{BA}^{(k)} \end{pmatrix}
\qquad \text{for } t \in [\tau_k,\, \tau_{k+1}),
\]
with grid edges \(\tau_0 < \tau_1 < \dots\) from dating.log_time_grid. A branch running from child time \(t_c\) to parent time \(t_p\) is split at the cell boundaries it crosses (dating.split_branch), so its transition matrix is a time-ordered product over the cells it spans:
\[
P_{\text{branch}}(t_c, t_p) \;=\; \prod_{k} \exp\!\big(Q_k\, d_k\big),
\qquad d_k = \text{duration of the branch inside cell } k,
\]
(dating.composite_transition). Recombination / tract structure is still carried entirely by edge span, exactly as in the homogeneous model — only the vertical generator gains time-dependence.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
Directional smooth M-step
Given \(D\) and \(J\), each direction’s rate is a penalised-Poisson log-spline: the per-cell jump count \(J[k, m, n]\) is Poisson with mean \(q_{mn}(t_k)\,D[k, m]\) (occupation in the source state is the exposure), and \(\log q_{mn}\) is a B-spline with a second-difference roughness penalty whose smoothing \(\lambda\) is chosen by GCV (dating.fit_poisson_spline, dating.select_lambda_gcv). Fitting \(q_{AB}\) and \(q_{BA}\) separately is what makes the estimator directional — it can see an asymmetric pulse.
The fit is restricted to the informative window (cells whose occupation exceeds a small fraction of the maximum); data-poor deep cells are flat-clamped rather than allowed to diverge (dating.directional_rate_splines). A smooth spline (rather than independent per-cell rates) is the natural fit because there is only power to resolve changes on a coalescent timescale — and it is flexible enough to capture genuinely abrupt cross-coalescence-rate changes where that power exists (verified before building; the “spline-resolution” exploration).

Direction convention
A jump is recorded parent → child = old → young = forward in time. So a backward-time admixture pulse of \(A\) into \(B\) appears as mass in \(q_{BA}(t)\), not \(q_{AB}(t)\) — worth keeping straight when reading a profile.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
The EM loop
fit_rate_through_time alternates the two:
init   q_AB(t) = q_BA(t) = const          # from one homogeneous tspaint.fit
E-step accumulate per-cell D, J under the current Q(t)   (decoupled fine-grid accumulation)
M-step refit q_AB(t), q_BA(t) as directional penalised-Poisson splines  ->  new Q(t)
repeat until the span-integrated log-likelihood converges
The log-likelihood is monotone (an EM correctness check, asserted in the tests). The payoff over a single global \(Q\) is a sharp rate-through-time: the per-cell rate is no longer smeared by one genome-wide constant.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
Using it
The headline entry point needs only a tree sequence and reference labels — the log-time grid is built automatically from the node ages:
import tspaint
rtt = tspaint.fit_rate_through_time(ts, labels)   # -> RateThroughTime
rtt.plot()                                       # q_AB(t), q_BA(t) on a log-time axis
Or reuse a painting’s fit (no second homogeneous fit; the painting is untouched):
painting = tspaint.paint(ts, labels)
rtt = painting.rate_through_time()               # warm-started from painting's (Q, π, w)

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
What it recovers
On a clean two-population split the cross-ancestry rate is ~0 more recently than the split (the two ancestries cannot share ancestry yet) and rises once \(t\) exceeds it — the dashed line is the true split time:









Figure 1: Directional cross-ancestry transition rates q_AB(t), q_BA(t) vs backward time: ~0 more recent than the population split, rising once cross-ancestry sharing becomes possible. The dashed line is the true split time.





Source: Admixture dating: cross-ancestry rate through time
Across the validation rungs (true ARG, strong structure) the estimator: has a sum-invariant E-step and a monotone EM loop; sharpens the onset toward the true split as it iterates; resolves demographic structure (a pulse, an asymmetric pulse, ongoing migration); and survives on an inferred ARG — SINGER samples reproduce the true-ARG profile (onset and median cross-coalescence time within ~1 % of truth), the binding axis there being calibration rather than point accuracy.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
Why not paint(dating=True)
Feeding the fitted \(Q(t)\) back into the painting (paint_qt) gives no measurable local-ancestry accuracy gain (~1.0 either way) — only sharper confidence, at roughly 4–5× the cost. That sharpening was measured only where accuracy is already ~1.0, so “sharper” there means “better calibrated.” But tspaint’s distinguishing value is honest calibration — its posteriors correctly relax toward 0.5 where the query↔︎reference signal is weak (see Results), and whether \(Q(t)\) preserves that relaxation in weak-signal regimes is unmeasured. A discoverable flag that quietly re-paints under \(Q(t)\) could trade away the very property that makes the painter worth using. So the paths stay separate: painting uses the homogeneous, calibrated \(Q\); dating is its own deliverable. The \(Q(t)\) re-paint remains available, opt-in, via dating.paint_qt.

title: Admixture dating: rate through time
text:
Caveats

Bounded by ARG quality, like painting — the profile is only as good as the genealogy and its node ages. SINGER’s coalescent-calibrated times help here and propagate uncertainty.
\(\pi\) held fixed (uniform), as everywhere in tspaint, to avoid the root-frequency degeneracy (see Assumptions).
The absolute rate scale depends on the time calibration of the input; the shape of the profile (where rates rise, their direction and relative magnitude) is the robust read.

title: Haplotype painting
text:
import tspaint
from tspaint.sim import SOURCE_A, SOURCE_B, ADMIXED, ANCESTRAL
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
import seaborn as sns
import demesdraw
import msprime
from vscodenb import set_vscode_theme, vscode_theme
set_vscode_theme()

Ne=2_000
T_admix=200.0
T_split=20_000
f_A=0.3
sequence_length = 5e6
recombination_rate = 1e-8
mutation_rate = 1.1e-8
ploidy = 2
random_seed = 1
infer = True

n_admix = 8
n_ref = 8

d = msprime.Demography()
d.add_population(name=SOURCE_A, initial_size=Ne)
d.add_population(name=SOURCE_B, initial_size=Ne)
d.add_population(name=ADMIXED, initial_size=Ne)
d.add_population(name=ANCESTRAL, initial_size=Ne)
# Admixed pop forms (backward in time) as a mixture of the two sources.
d.add_admixture(time=T_admix, derived=ADMIXED,
                ancestral=[SOURCE_A, SOURCE_B], proportions=[f_A, 1.0 - f_A])
# Census after the pulse: every lineage is now in A or B; census nodes label
# each lineage's source per genomic segment (the local-ancestry truth).
d.add_census(time=T_admix + 1)
# Sources coalesce into a common ancestor deeper in time.
d.add_population_split(time=T_split, derived=[SOURCE_A, SOURCE_B],
                        ancestral=ANCESTRAL)
graph = msprime.Demography.to_demes(d)
with vscode_theme(style='ticks'):
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 3))
    demesdraw.tubes(graph, ax=ax, seed=1)

title: Haplotype painting
text:
Simulate

ts = msprime.sim_ancestry(
        samples={ADMIXED: n_admix, SOURCE_A: n_ref, SOURCE_B: n_ref},
        demography=d,
        sequence_length=sequence_length,
        recombination_rate=recombination_rate,
        ploidy=ploidy,
        random_seed=random_seed,
    )

pop = ts.tables.nodes.population
name = {p: ts.population(p).metadata.get("name", str(p)) for p in range(ts.num_populations)}
A = next(p for p, n in name.items() if n == SOURCE_A)
B = next(p for p, n in name.items() if n == SOURCE_B)
admix = next(p for p, n in name.items() if n == ADMIXED)
sop = {A: 0, B: 1}
labels = {int(s): sop[pop[s]] for s in ts.samples() if pop[s] in (A, B)}
queries = [int(s) for s in ts.samples() if pop[s] == admix]
truth = tspaint.metrics.map_truth({q: tspaint.local_ancestry_truth(ts)[0][q] for q in queries}, sop)
work = ts
if infer:
    work = tspaint.io.tsinfer(
        tspaint.io.add_mutations(
            ts, rate=mutation_rate, random_seed=random_seed
            )
        )
    # work = tspaint.io.singer(
    #     tspaint.io.add_mutations(
    #         ts, rate=mutation_rate, random_seed=random_seed
    #         ),
    #         Ne=Ne, recombination_rate=recombination_rate, mutation_rate=mutation_rate
    #     )

title: Haplotype painting
text:
Paint

painting = tspaint.paint(ts, labels)

title: Haplotype painting
text:
Plot

sns.set_style("ticks")

L = ts.sequence_length
qs = painting.queries
segments = painting.segments(deadband=0.4)

sm = matplotlib.cm.ScalarMappable(norm=matplotlib.colors.Normalize(0, 1), cmap='coolwarm')
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9, 0.3 * len(qs) + 1))
gs = fig.add_gridspec(len(qs), 2, width_ratios=[1,0.03], hspace=0)
axes = [fig.add_subplot(gs[i, 0]) for i in range(len(qs))]

for i, q in enumerate(qs):
    for (l, r, s) in truth[q]:
        axes[i].barh(-0.25, r - l, left=l, height=0.5,
                color=sm.to_rgba(1.0 if s == 0 else 0.0), edgecolor="none")
    for (l, r, s) in segments[q]:
        axes[i].barh(0.25, r - l, left=l, height=0.5,
                color=sm.to_rgba(1.0 if s == 0 else 0.0), edgecolor="none")
    for seg in painting.posteriors[q]:
        axes[i].barh(1, seg.right - seg.left, left=seg.left, height=1,
                color=sm.to_rgba(seg.posterior[0]), edgecolor="none")
    axes[i].set_ylim(-0.5, 1.5)
    axes[i].set_xlim(0, L)
    axes[i].set_ylabel('hapl. name', rotation=0, fontsize=7, color="1.0", horizontalalignment="right")
    axes[i].yaxis.set_major_locator(ticker.NullLocator())
    if i < len(axes)-1:
        axes[i].xaxis.set_major_locator(ticker.NullLocator())

ax = fig.add_subplot(gs[:, 1])
ax.set_axis_off()
cb = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, fraction=0.5, pad=0.01)
cb.set_label("P(ancestry A)")

plt.tight_layout()

title: Admixture dating: cross-ancestry rate through time
text:
q_AB(t) and q_BA(t) vs (backward) time on a log axis. The rise marks the divergence epoch; recent time carries ~no cross-ancestry rate. (Convention: a jump is parent→child = old→young = forward in time, so a backward-time A→B admixture shows in q_BA.)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.2, 4))
rtt.plot(ax=ax)
ax.axvline(T_split, color="k", ls="--", lw=1, label=f"true split ({T_split})")
ax.set_title("Cross-ancestry rate through time"); ax.legend(fontsize=8); fig.tight_layout()
fig.show()

title: Calibration & accuracy vs admixture age
text:
tspaint’s edge is a calibrated soft posterior. Here we show the reliability of P(A) and how discrimination decays as admixture ages (the reference signal is lost when admixed lineages coalesce among themselves before an old pulse — a coalescent limit, not tree-inference error).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import cm, colors
import tspaint
from tspaint.sim import SOURCE_A, SOURCE_B, ADMIXED

plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 110, "font.size": 10, "axes.spines.top": False,
                     "axes.spines.right": False})

def admixture(n_admix=8, n_ref=8, L=2e6, T_admix=100, Ne=1000, T_split=5000, f_A=0.5,
              seed=1, infer=False, mutation_rate=4e-7):
    """Simulate admixture with known truth; return (ts, labels, queries, truth_states)."""
    ts = tspaint.simulate_admixture(n_admix=n_admix, n_ref=n_ref, sequence_length=L,
                                  recombination_rate=1e-8, random_seed=seed, Ne=Ne,
                                  T_admix=T_admix, T_split=T_split, f_A=f_A)
    pop = ts.tables.nodes.population
    name = {p: ts.population(p).metadata.get("name", str(p)) for p in range(ts.num_populations)}
    A = next(p for p, n in name.items() if n == SOURCE_A)
    B = next(p for p, n in name.items() if n == SOURCE_B)
    admix = next(p for p, n in name.items() if n == ADMIXED)
    sop = {A: 0, B: 1}
    labels = {int(s): sop[pop[s]] for s in ts.samples() if pop[s] in (A, B)}
    queries = [int(s) for s in ts.samples() if pop[s] == admix]
    truth = tspaint.metrics.map_truth({q: tspaint.local_ancestry_truth(ts)[0][q] for q in queries}, sop)
    work = ts
    if infer:
        work = tspaint.io.infer_tree_sequence(tspaint.io.add_mutations(ts, rate=mutation_rate,
                                                                   random_seed=seed))
    return work, labels, queries, truth

CMAP = "RdBu_r"   # red = ancestry A (state 0), blue = ancestry B (state 1)

def plot_painting(painting, truth, ts, title="", segments=None):
    """Soft posterior P(A) painted along the genome per query haplotype, with a thin truth
    strip beneath each. If `segments` (hard tracts) is given, draw those instead of soft."""
    qs = painting.queries
    L = ts.sequence_length
    sm = cm.ScalarMappable(norm=colors.Normalize(0, 1), cmap=CMAP)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 0.42 * len(qs) + 1.2))
    for i, q in enumerate(qs):
        if segments is None:
            for seg in painting.posteriors[q]:
                ax.barh(i, seg.right - seg.left, left=seg.left, height=0.74,
                        color=sm.to_rgba(seg.posterior[0]), edgecolor="none")
        else:
            for (l, r, s) in segments[q]:
                ax.barh(i, r - l, left=l, height=0.74,
                        color=sm.to_rgba(1.0 if s == 0 else 0.0), edgecolor="none")
        for (l, r, s) in truth[q]:
            ax.barh(i - 0.46, r - l, left=l, height=0.13,
                    color=sm.to_rgba(1.0 if s == 0 else 0.0), edgecolor="none")
    ax.set_xlim(0, L); ax.set_ylim(-0.8, len(qs) - 0.2)
    ax.set_yticks(range(len(qs))); ax.set_yticklabels([f"hap {q}" for q in qs], fontsize=8)
    ax.set_xlabel("genomic position (bp)"); ax.set_title(title)
    cb = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, fraction=0.025, pad=0.01)
    cb.set_label("P(ancestry A)")
    ax.text(0, len(qs) - 0.5, "thin strip below each = true ancestry", fontsize=7, color="0.4")
    fig.tight_layout()
    return fig



Predicted vs empirical P(A), span-weighted by probability bin (pooled over several seeds for a smooth curve). A calibrated painter lies on the diagonal.

import numpy as np
pred, emp, wt = [], [], []
for seed in range(1, 7):
    ts, labels, queries, truth = admixture(n_admix=8, n_ref=8, L=1e6, T_admix=200, seed=seed)
    p = tspaint.paint(ts, labels)
    rc = tspaint.metrics.reliability_curve(p.posteriors, truth, state=0, n_bins=10)
    pred.append(rc["pred"]); emp.append(rc["emp"]); wt.append(rc["weight"])
# weighted average across seeds, per bin
pred = np.concatenate(pred); emp = np.concatenate(emp); wt = np.concatenate(wt)
order = np.argsort(pred)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.6, 4.4))
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "k--", lw=1, label="perfect calibration")
ax.scatter(pred[order], emp[order], s=12 + 200 * wt[order] / wt.max(), alpha=0.6, color="C3")
ax.set_xlabel("predicted P(A)"); ax.set_ylabel("empirical P(A)")
ax.set_title("Reliability"); ax.legend(fontsize=8); fig.tight_layout()
fig.show()

/var/folders/s6/srs8qkh52w1_h32d65z95tth0000gn/T/ipykernel_78981/1452454447.py:18: UserWarning: FigureCanvasAgg is non-interactive, and thus cannot be shown
  fig.show()








Figure 1: Reliability diagram: predicted vs empirical P(ancestry A); marker size ∝ span weight. Points near the diagonal indicate calibration.








Balanced accuracy and mean confidence as the admixture pulse ages (deep split, so only the query↔︎reference link — not tract length — varies).

ages = [30, 100, 300, 1000, 3000]
rows = tspaint.experiments.age_sweep(ages, n_admix=8, n_ref=8, sequence_length=1e6,
                                   Ne=1000, T_split=8000, f_A=0.5, seed=1, max_iter=8)
ba = [r["balanced_accuracy"] for r in rows]; cf = [r["confidence"] for r in rows]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 3.8))
ax.plot(ages, ba, "o-", label="balanced accuracy")
ax.plot(ages, cf, "s--", label="mean confidence", color="C1")
ax.axhline(0.5, color="0.6", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_xlabel("admixture age (generations)"); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02)
ax.set_title("Discrimination vs admixture age"); ax.legend(fontsize=8); fig.tight_layout()
fig.show()

Figure 2: Balanced accuracy and confidence vs admixture age: tspaint discriminates well at recent–moderate admixture; the reference signal is lost at old admixture under present-day sampling.

title: Calibration & accuracy vs admixture age
text:
Predicted vs empirical P(A), span-weighted by probability bin (pooled over several seeds for a smooth curve). A calibrated painter lies on the diagonal.

import numpy as np
pred, emp, wt = [], [], []
for seed in range(1, 7):
    ts, labels, queries, truth = admixture(n_admix=8, n_ref=8, L=1e6, T_admix=200, seed=seed)
    p = tspaint.paint(ts, labels)
    rc = tspaint.metrics.reliability_curve(p.posteriors, truth, state=0, n_bins=10)
    pred.append(rc["pred"]); emp.append(rc["emp"]); wt.append(rc["weight"])
# weighted average across seeds, per bin
pred = np.concatenate(pred); emp = np.concatenate(emp); wt = np.concatenate(wt)
order = np.argsort(pred)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.6, 4.4))
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "k--", lw=1, label="perfect calibration")
ax.scatter(pred[order], emp[order], s=12 + 200 * wt[order] / wt.max(), alpha=0.6, color="C3")
ax.set_xlabel("predicted P(A)"); ax.set_ylabel("empirical P(A)")
ax.set_title("Reliability"); ax.legend(fontsize=8); fig.tight_layout()
fig.show()

title: Calibration & accuracy vs admixture age
text:
Balanced accuracy and mean confidence as the admixture pulse ages (deep split, so only the query↔︎reference link — not tract length — varies).

ages = [30, 100, 300, 1000, 3000]
rows = tspaint.experiments.age_sweep(ages, n_admix=8, n_ref=8, sequence_length=1e6,
                                   Ne=1000, T_split=8000, f_A=0.5, seed=1, max_iter=8)
ba = [r["balanced_accuracy"] for r in rows]; cf = [r["confidence"] for r in rows]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 3.8))
ax.plot(ages, ba, "o-", label="balanced accuracy")
ax.plot(ages, cf, "s--", label="mean confidence", color="C1")
ax.axhline(0.5, color="0.6", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_xlabel("admixture age (generations)"); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02)
ax.set_title("Discrimination vs admixture age"); ax.legend(fontsize=8); fig.tight_layout()
fig.show()
